title: Weekly links July 10: micro vs macro approaches to poverty reduction, migration and development in a small country, financial education years later, and more…
gist: A development-economics link roundup covering the RCT micro-vs-macro debate, a new Review of Economic Studies Insights journal, striking long-term results from a Brazil high-school financial-education RCT, and climate-driven migration and fisheries dependence in Tuvalu and Kiribati.

insights.claim: Dean Karlan argues microeconomists cannot say how to make a country rich but can identify which candidate strategies best get kids into school, keep them healthy, or move them into the workforce, with large money riding on non-obvious answers.
insights.speaker: David McKenzie
insights.so_what: Frames the enduring case for RCT-based program selection over grand macro strategy, relevant to how development capital is allocated and evaluated.
insights.quote: A microeconomist can’t tell you how to make Kenya rich. But she can tell you which of several candidate strategies, based on prior theory and evidence, is most likely to get more kids into school
insights.locator: Dean Karlan now has a substack

insights.claim: The Review of Economic Studies is launching a new Insights journal publishing both 6,000-word Insights papers (like AER Insights) and a novel 'Communications' category of up to 2,000 words and two exhibits with a fast up-or-out editorial process.
insights.speaker: David McKenzie
insights.so_what: A faster short-form economics publishing channel could accelerate the flow of policy-relevant empirical findings into public debate.
insights.locator: The Review of Economic Studies is launching

insights.claim: A Brazil RCT of high-school financial education with ~25,000 students in 892 public high schools found participants borrow more prudently in the long run, using less credit overall and especially less high-interest credit-card debt and overdrafts.
insights.speaker: David McKenzie
insights.so_what: Rare long-horizon evidence that financial education durably shifts borrowing behavior, relevant to consumer-credit risk and financial-inclusion program design.
insights.quote: financial education leads to more prudent borrowing decisions in the long run
insights.locator: On VoxDev, Miriam Bruhn and co-authors

insights.claim: Eight to nine years after graduation, Brazilian students exposed to high-school financial education are 10.2% more likely to own a microenterprise and 4.8% less likely to hold a formal salaried job.
insights.speaker: David McKenzie
insights.so_what: Curriculum design can measurably shift young adults toward entrepreneurship over formal employment, a lever for SME formation and a caution on labor-market effects.
insights.quote: participants are 10.2% more likely to own a microenterprise and 4.8% less likely to hold a formal salaried job
insights.locator: On VoxDev, Miriam Bruhn and co-authors

themes.theme: Climate migration and fisheries in the Pacific

insights.claim: Tuvalu, an archipelagic state with an estimated 2026 population of just 9,420, is at the forefront of the climate crisis and is shifting away from seafaring toward temporary and permanent migration to New Zealand and Australia.
insights.speaker: David McKenzie
insights.so_what: Managed migration as a climate-resilience strategy for micro-states is a template for policy and a signal of labor-mobility corridors opening in the Pacific.
insights.quote: Temporary and permanent migration pathways are seen as important components of the country’s overall strategy to build climate resilience

insights.claim: People in Kiribati and Tuvalu consume 72 kg and 56 kg of fish per year respectively, among the highest rates in the world, with fisheries the primary income activity for 47% of Kiribati households (67% rural) and 25% of Tuvalu households (37% rural).
insights.speaker: David McKenzie
insights.so_what: Extreme fisheries dependence makes these economies acutely exposed to climate-driven fish-stock shifts, not just sea-level rise, a food-security and livelihood risk.
insights.quote: people in Kiribati and Tuvalu consume 72 kg and 56 kg of fish per year, respectively—among the highest consumption rates in the world
insights.locator: on the Global Dev blog, Michelle DeFreese

insights.claim: Climatic events affecting fish stocks, not only rising sea levels, are having material impacts on Pacific island economies dependent on fishing for food and income.
insights.speaker: David McKenzie
insights.so_what: Broadens climate-risk assessment for small island states beyond inundation to marine-ecosystem disruption affecting the core income base.
insights.locator: on the Global Dev blog, Michelle DeFreese
